title: 72 Hours

jokes.text: No. See, when I laugh at you, it's more like this... [followed by exaggerated demonstration laugh]
jokes.setup: Carlton thinks Will was laughing at him during their musical performance
jokes.punchline: Will demonstrates the difference with an exaggerated, theatrical laugh
jokes.explanation: Strong character moment showing Will's theatrical personality and physical comedy timing

jokes.text: Man, y'all butchered that song. Hey, C, I'm telling you, if the Commodores heard you singing... they'd tie you down to the tracks and run you over with the soul train.
jokes.setup: Criticizing Carlton's singing ability
jokes.punchline: Visual pun combining 'soul train' (the music concept) with literal train tracks
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that works on multiple levels - references Soul Train show while creating vivid comedic imagery

jokes.text: All right, but just out of curiosity, Carlton, what color are you?
jokes.setup: Carlton says Will is 'green' with jealousy
jokes.punchline: Will deflects by questioning Carlton's own 'color' - his authenticity
jokes.explanation: Sharp comeback that introduces the episode's central theme about racial identity and authenticity

jokes.text: No. It's that tie that does it.
jokes.setup: Carlton defending his blackness despite his upbringing and speech patterns
jokes.punchline: Will points to Carlton's presumably preppy tie as the real issue
jokes.explanation: Efficient visual callback to Carlton's preppy appearance, perfect economy of words

jokes.text: And if you can make it there, you'll make it anywhere.
jokes.setup: Discussion about surviving in Compton
jokes.punchline: Parodies 'New York, New York' lyrics but applied to the tough neighborhood of Compton
jokes.explanation: Clever cultural reference that works as both parody and street credibility joke

jokes.text: I don't know. That's my whole reason for living.
jokes.setup: Carlton asking Will to stop making cheap shots at him
jokes.punchline: Will deadpan admits that mocking Carlton is his life's purpose
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Will's honesty about his relationship with Carlton delivered with comedic timing

jokes.text: Damn! Why didn't we bet money?
jokes.setup: Carlton confidently accepts the Compton challenge
jokes.punchline: Will realizes he missed an easy opportunity to profit from Carlton's naivety
jokes.explanation: Natural reaction that shows Will's opportunistic side while setting up expectations

jokes.text: Excellent, Miss Hilary. You must let me know what that feels like.
jokes.setup: Hilary mentions getting a raise at the art gallery
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey dryly requests to know what getting a raise feels like (implying he's underpaid)
jokes.explanation: Classic Geoffrey dry wit - perfectly delivered class commentary disguised as politeness

jokes.text: It even gave me the willies when Beaver used to do it.
jokes.setup: Carlton saying he's never lied to his parents
jokes.punchline: References 'Leave it to Beaver' - showing his wholesome, old-fashioned mindset
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton character moment - the reference to classic TV shows his innocent, sheltered perspective

jokes.text: Man, it's about to be Diss-fest '91 around here.
jokes.setup: Carlton backing down from the bet
jokes.punchline: Will creates a mock festival name for the upcoming roast session
jokes.explanation: Creative wordplay that captures both the time period (1991) and Will's theatrical personality

jokes.text: I found it in the trash can, underneath a banana.
jokes.setup: Uncle Phil claiming he didn't forget to sign permission slips
jokes.punchline: Carlton reveals the specific, embarrassing location where he found them
jokes.explanation: Specific visual detail makes Uncle Phil's forgetfulness more embarrassing and concrete

jokes.text: I'm going straight to hell.
jokes.setup: Carlton agreeing to lie to his parents about the trip
jokes.punchline: His dramatic, guilt-ridden reaction to telling one small lie
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton - his extreme guilt over a white lie shows his sheltered, morally rigid upbringing

jokes.text: Crib: House, dwelling, domicile.
jokes.setup: Will uses street slang 'crib' for house
jokes.punchline: Carlton translates it like he's reading from a dictionary
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy showing Carlton's formal, academic approach to street culture

jokes.text: Hip-hop flash cards? I made them myself. I've been cramming all night.
jokes.setup: Will asking how Carlton knows street slang
jokes.punchline: Carlton's academic approach to learning street culture through homemade study materials
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy - Carlton treating street culture like an academic subject is both absurd and perfectly in character

jokes.text: I just hope I can get it out of my head before I take the SATs.
jokes.setup: Carlton studying hip-hop slang
jokes.punchline: His worry that street knowledge will interfere with his academic performance
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation showing Carlton's priorities - treats street culture as academic contamination

jokes.text: That's called leaving me hanging... because my hand is literally left hanging in the air.
jokes.setup: Carlton tries to give Jazz a high-five but gets ignored
jokes.punchline: Carlton explains the idiom with literal precision
jokes.explanation: Classic Carlton over-explanation of street culture, treating slang with academic literalism

jokes.text: Let me have my butler get them. Sorry. I crack myself up.
jokes.setup: Carlton asking where to put his luggage in Jazz's modest apartment
jokes.punchline: Jazz sarcastically offers his non-existent butler, then laughs at his own joke
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Jazz, showing his awareness of the class difference with self-deprecating humor

jokes.text: You can put them on the floor, but watch out for my carpet... 'cause it's imported from Persia. I could do this all day.
jokes.setup: Jazz continuing to mock Carlton's wealth
jokes.punchline: Claims his basic carpet is imported Persian, then admits he's enjoying the sarcasm
jokes.explanation: Continues the sarcastic thread with escalating absurdity about his modest living situation

jokes.text: Applegate? That sounds like a school word. I don't like school words. I can't applegate myself to them.
jokes.setup: Carlton uses the word 'acclimate' which Tiny mishears
jokes.punchline: Tiny creates a malapropism by using 'applegate' as a verb while rejecting 'school words'
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay showing the character's anti-intellectual stance while creating accidental comedy

jokes.text: Everybody, put your hands in the air! [followed by awkward silence and confused reactions]
jokes.setup: Sounds like a robbery or police raid
jokes.punchline: Context suggests it's something mundane, creating false alarm comedy
jokes.explanation: Standard misdirection gag playing on expectations but lacking follow-through

jokes.text: 20 cents. That's not a real Gucci bag, is it? Maybe it is. Maybe it ain't.
jokes.setup: Vendor desperately lowering price on fake Gucci bag
jokes.punchline: Price drops to almost nothing, vendor maintains mysterious stance about authenticity
jokes.explanation: Good escalation comedy with absurd price drops, though character integration is minimal

jokes.text: Maybe if you went out and bought a well-tailored suit... and lost the earring, then you'd look like a real salesman. And that grammar...
jokes.setup: Carlton trying to give street vendor business advice
jokes.punchline: His completely tone-deaf suggestions reveal his privileged, sheltered perspective
jokes.explanation: Strong cringe comedy showing Carlton's complete misunderstanding of street economics and culture

jokes.text: I spent half the ride trying to convince him not to say, 'Dyn-o-mite.'
jokes.setup: Will expressing concern about Carlton fitting in
jokes.punchline: Reveals Carlton wanted to use outdated '70s catchphrase to seem 'street'
jokes.explanation: Perfect insight into Carlton's misguided attempts at street credibility through outdated references

jokes.text: The Detroit Lions still got those cheerleaders?
jokes.setup: Will mentions 'tossing Carlton to the lions'
jokes.punchline: Jazz misunderstands the metaphor, thinking about actual Detroit Lions cheerleaders
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing Jazz's distractible, simple nature through literal interpretation

jokes.text: Carlton, you look like a pirate. Yo, stop fronting. You know this gear is chill.
jokes.setup: Carlton has transformed his appearance to look more 'street'
jokes.punchline: His attempt at street fashion makes him look like a pirate; he defends it with forced slang
jokes.explanation: Strong visual comedy with Carlton's costume change combined with his awkward adoption of street slang

jokes.text: Hey, Prince, lay off of him, man. We like C-note.
jokes.setup: Will is mocking Carlton's transformation
jokes.punchline: Jazz and crew have actually warmed to Carlton and given him a street nickname
jokes.explanation: Good ironic turn - Carlton is actually succeeding at fitting in, subverting Will's expectations.

jokes.text: Yo, how many times do I gotta tell you? Write your questions down. I'll hook you up later.
jokes.setup: Carlton being asked about business/financial advice
jokes.punchline: He's become their informal financial advisor, adopting street speech patterns awkwardly
jokes.explanation: Good character development showing Carlton finding his niche by combining his skills with street culture

jokes.text: I'm going to get me some of them douche marks. That's deutsche marks.
jokes.setup: Guy mispronounces 'deutsche marks' as 'douche marks'
jokes.punchline: Carlton politely corrects the pronunciation
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective malapropism with Carlton's characteristic need to educate

jokes.text: Sorry. There's no hip-hop word for sales bonanza.
jokes.setup: Carlton using business terminology
jokes.punchline: He apologizes for not having street slang equivalent for business terms
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the flash cards joke while showing Carlton's continuing effort to translate between worlds

jokes.text: It's not that kind of park, Boo Boo.
jokes.setup: Carlton mentions calling park rangers for protection
jokes.punchline: Will's condescending nickname while explaining this isn't Jellystone Park
jokes.explanation: Perfect condescending nickname that captures Carlton's naive view of urban dangers

jokes.text: Geoffrey, did you really enjoy having Will out of the house that much? The closest I'll ever get to a bonus.
jokes.setup: Vivian asking if Geoffrey enjoyed Will's absence
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's dry admission that Will's absence is like getting a workplace bonus
jokes.explanation: Classic Geoffrey wit - perfectly timed deadpan delivery with implicit commentary on his working conditions

jokes.text: I can get $20 for this china dog. $2.
jokes.setup: Carlton now trying to sell household items
jokes.punchline: Immediately drops price dramatically, copying the street vendor technique
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Carlton has internalized the wrong business lessons from the street

jokes.text: This is the police! Carlton, come out with your hands up. You'll never take me alive.
jokes.setup: Uncle Phil pretending to be police to get Carlton's attention
jokes.punchline: Carlton dramatically declares he'll never be taken alive
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Carlton's transformation into a 'criminal' mindset, plus Philip's creative parenting

jokes.text: On reflection, perhaps I should.
jokes.setup: Vivian suggesting Geoffrey join them for church
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's formal consideration suggests he needs divine intervention (possibly regarding his job)
jokes.explanation: Subtle Geoffrey humor implying he needs spiritual help with his current life situation

jokes.text: The back row? The light is completely different there. I have to change.
jokes.setup: Family trying to hurry to church, might have to sit in back
jokes.punchline: Hilary's vanity requires outfit change based on seating lighting
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary moment - her vanity extends even to church seating arrangements

jokes.text: If a brother comes between a brother and his brothers... then that brother's considered a mother.
jokes.setup: Will explaining why he can't interfere with Carlton's situation
jokes.punchline: Convoluted street logic using 'brother/mother' wordplay
jokes.explanation: Creative wordplay with street philosophy, though slightly convoluted in delivery

jokes.text: I'll tell you who's a mother: Me. And I am going there and I am bringing my baby back.
jokes.setup: Will's convoluted explanation about 'mothers'
jokes.punchline: Vivian cuts through the nonsense with literal maternal authority
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback and escalation - Vivian's maternal authority trumps street logic

jokes.text: Geoffrey fell down the back steps, split his wig. We gotta go to the hospital.
jokes.setup: Will trying to get Carlton to leave by creating emergency
jokes.punchline: Uses street slang 'split his wig' for head injury in fake emergency
jokes.explanation: Will adapting his lie-telling to use street vernacular shows his code-switching ability

jokes.text: Carlton was acting in what? In Cosby?
jokes.setup: Will says Carlton was acting 'irresponsibly'
jokes.punchline: Friend mishears/transforms it into 'Cosby' reference
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor referencing competing Black sitcom while playing on mishearing of 'irresponsibly'

jokes.text: Prince used a school word. You 5-0'd your own cousin.
jokes.setup: Will using sophisticated vocabulary
jokes.punchline: The guys turn on Will for using 'school words' and calling the parents
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and role reversal - now Will is the outsider for his vocabulary and actions

jokes.text: No, but I think I heard Uncle Phil upstairs testing his belts out.
jokes.setup: Carlton asking if parents have talked to Will yet
jokes.punchline: Will implies Uncle Phil is preparing physical punishment
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about corporal punishment that fits Will's street background and current fear

jokes.text: What, are you kidding? They're going to kill you guys.
jokes.setup: Hilary seeming to comfort the boys about their punishment
jokes.punchline: Complete reversal - she was just teasing them before dropping the harsh truth
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection with Hilary's fake sympathy followed by blunt honesty

jokes.text: There's no 'yo' at the end of it. I'm pretty sure of it.
jokes.setup: Uncle Phil mockingly imitating Will's speech patterns
jokes.punchline: Will corrects the grammar of his own slang being mocked
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor with Will correcting the accuracy of his own stereotyped speech patterns

jokes.text: Peace out.
jokes.setup: Will just corrected Uncle Phil's street impression
jokes.punchline: Immediately uses street slang to exit, undermining his correction
jokes.explanation: Perfect immediate callback that undercuts Will's linguistic correction with self-contradictory behavior

jokes.text: Differently.
jokes.setup: Will says Carlton treats him like an idiot because he talks 'different'
jokes.punchline: Carlton automatically corrects Will's grammar mid-argument
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Carlton can't help but correct grammar even during emotional confrontation

jokes.text: You love me. My brother, you wanna take this outside?
jokes.setup: Carlton declaring that Will loves him
jokes.punchline: Will responds to declaration of love with street challenge to fight
jokes.explanation: Great callback and escalation - Will's tough-guy response to emotional vulnerability creates perfect ironic ending

tmdb_overview: Will challenges Carlton to spend time in a tough neighborhood, trying to survive by masquerading as a financial consultant.
